General safety and regulatory information
Conventions for safety and regulatory information
1.1 Conventions for safety and regulatory information
1.1.1 Conventions for warnings, cautions, and notes
This is the convention for the warnings, cautions, and the content of notes in the technical
documentation for Elekta oncology products.
The conventions give information about the class and the level of the risk in the warnings and
cautions, and the content of notes.
WARNING x.x
A warning is information about procedures, tasks, or conditions with a risk of
fatal or serious injury, injury, or clinical mistreatment, if you do not obey the
instructions.
CAUTION x.x
A caution is information about procedures, tasks, or conditions with a risk of
damage to the equipment, data, or the environment, if you do not obey the
instructions.
Note: A note gives more information about a related text.

General safety and regulatory information
Safety precautions
1.4.1 Important safety instructions
This is a safety instruction for the protection of the User and patients during the installation,
operation, and maintenance of the equipment.
It is important to install, operate, and do the maintenance correctly on all the equipment. This is
most important for safety related items. For the safety of the User and patients, Elekta
recommends that the User read, understand, and obey:
• All warnings, cautions, and notes in this document and related documents
• All warnings, cautions, and safety markings on the equipment and the accessories of the
equipment
• All safety instructions and information in this document and related documents.
Elekta recommends that:
• All Users obey the precautions in this section
• All Users examine the warnings, cautions, and notes in this document and use them in
their local Work Instructions.
Before the User operates, or do work on the equipment, it is important that they read and
understand the emergency procedures.
1.4.2 Using this document
This is a description of how to use this document.
WARNING1.2
Do not use this document to operate the equipment or to do the repair and
maintenance work. If you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury or clinical
mistreatment.
Only use this document together with the applicable user and service documentation. Before you
operate the equipment or do the repair and maintenance work, read the warnings, cautions and
safety precautions in the Instructions for Use and corrective maintenance documentation.
1.4.3 Safety precautions for the compatibility of the equipment
These are the precautions for the operation of the equipment with parts or accessories not approved
by Elekta.
WARNING1.3
Do not use parts or accessories unless they are supplied or approved by Elekta. If
you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury, clinical mistreatment, and
damage to the equipment.
Only operate the equipment with Elekta supplied or approved, compatible equipment or parts.
Accessories, transducers, and cables that are not specified by or do not have the approval of Elekta
can cause an incorrect treatment delivery. They can also have an effect on the performance of the
electromagnetic compatibility (EMC), which can increase the emissions or decrease the immunity
of the equipment.
Contact Elekta for information about the compatibility of other equipment or parts.

General safety and regulatory information
Safety precautions
1.4.4 Safety precautions for the electromagnetic compatibility
These are safety precautions to prevent an unwanted effect on the operation of the equipment by
the radio frequency emissions.
The design and manufacture of Elekta products is in compliance with applicable EMC standards.
The intended result of the standards is to minimize the risk of electromagnetic interference by RF
devices. The type of device and the distance to the equipment has an effect on the intensity of the
electromagnetic interference. The equipment includes the linear accelerator and accessories, the
control cabinets, the computers in the control room, and equipment from other manufacturer.
It is necessary to have special precautions for EMC emissions. Elekta recommends that you obey
these precautions when you use RF devices near the equipment.
• Do not use RF devices unless they are in compliance with international standards for
electromagnetic compatibility, for example, the CE or FCC mark.
• Do not use a two-way emergency radio in the treatment room or the control room. Only
use an emergency radio if there is an emergency.
• Do not use cellular telephones, or other devices with cellular modems near the
equipment. The Instructions for Use contains information on the minimum safe distance
of these devices from the equipment.
• When you use a device that is enabled for wireless operation, it must be a minimum of
10 cm from the equipment.
• Radio-Frequency Identification (RFID) readers, when activated to do a scan of a RFID tag,
must be a minimum of one meter from the equipment.
Note: The RFID systems that Elekta supply use lower power levels. You can safely use these RFID systems
when you obey the procedures in the Instructions for Use for the product.
The system must be installed, put into service, and maintained in compliance with the EMC
information in the Instructions for Use.
Contact Elekta for information about the compatibility of parts and equipment.
WARNING1.4
Do not put portable or mobile radio frequency (RF) communication devices near to
the equipment. The emissions from such RF devices can be more than the EMC
standards, and can have unwanted effects on the equipment operation. If you
ignore this warning, the RF emissions can cause the equipment to have a
malfunction. A malfunction can cause fatal injury, clinical mistreatment, or damage
to the equipment.
WARNING1.5
Do not use accessories, transducers, and cables that are not specified by Elekta.
They can have an effect on the EMC performance, which can increase EMC
emissions or decrease the immunity to RF interference of the equipment. If you
ignore this warning, incorrect radiation delivery and other malfunctions can occur,
which can cause fatal injury or clinical mistreatment.

General safety and regulatory information
Safety precautions
1.4.5 Safety precautions for operation of the equipment
These are safety precautions to prevent incorrect radiation delivery and other malfunctions.
WARNING1.6
Do not operate the equipment unless the QA checks are satisfactorily completed.
If you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury, clinical mistreatment, and
damage to the equipment.
WARNING1.7
Do not operate the equipment if it is damaged, defective, or incorrectly calibrated.
If you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury, clinical mistreatment, and
damage to the equipment.
Only operate the equipment if you are a qualified person with the applicable authority. Such
operation must apply best practice for clinical treatment and obey the laws and regulations in the
jurisdiction in which the equipment is installed.
Before you operate the equipment, make sure that:
• There are no defective parts.
• The equipment calibration is correct.
• The applicable QA checks are satisfactorily completed.
• The user routine checks are satisfactorily completed.
• The planned maintenance schedule is completed satisfactorily.
For information on the user routine checks and the planned maintenance schedule, see the
applicable section of the Instructions for Use.
It is the responsibility of the User to set up a QA program that gives safe and satisfactory system
operation.
1.4.6 Safety precaution for Service Tools and lifting equipment
This is a safety precaution to prevent incorrect calibration and other malfunctions, which can cause
fatal injury and damage to the equipment.
WARNING1.8
Do not use incorrectly calibrated or defective Service Tools. If you ignore this
warning, you can cause injury and damage to the equipment.
WARNING1.9
Do not use defective or damaged lifting equipment. If you ignore this warning, you
can cause fatal injury and damage to the equipment.
Before you use the Service Tool or lifting equipment, make sure that:
Elekta Medical Linear Accelerator Document ID:4513 370 2185 07

General safety and regulatory information
Safety precautions
• There are no defective or damaged parts
• The calibration of the Service Tool is correct
• The applicable calibration certificate is valid
• The lifting equipment gives the maximum Safe Working Load (SWL).
For information on the maintenance and calibration schedules of the Service Tool, see the
Instructions for Use and Calibration Certificate for the tool.
1.4.7 Safety precaution for work with safety devices during clinical
operation
This is a safety precaution to prevent incorrect radiation delivery and other safety risks during
clinical operation.
WARNING1.10
Do not remove, change, or override the safety switches, interlocks, and other
safety devices. If you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury or clinical
mistreatment.
Elekta install the safety switches, interlocks, and other safety devices to give the necessary
protection against incorrect treatment delivery and other safety risks during clinical operation.
1.4.8 Safety precaution for work with safety devices during service
operation
This is a safety precautions to prevent incorrect procedures during repair, and other safety risks,
which can cause fatal or serious injury.
WARNING1.11
Do not remove, change, or override the safety switches, interlocks, or other safety
devices unless special instructions in this document tell you to. If you ignore this
warning, you can cause fatal injury.
The procedures in this document can tell you to remove, change, or override the safety switches,
interlocks, or other safety devices during repair or maintenance work. When the work is
completed, make all the safety devices active again, and do a test of them.
1.4.9 Safety precaution using new or unusual treatment techniques
This is a precaution to prevent an incorrect radiation delivery and other malfunctions during
treatments with new or unusual treatment techniques.
WARNING1.12
Do not use new or unusual treatment techniques unless the equipment has the
performance and correct configuration for delivery of the treatment. If you ignore
this warning, you can cause fatal injury and clinical mistreatment.
Document ID: 4513 370 2185 07 Elekta Medical Linear Accelerator
Corrective Maintenance Manual Technical Reference
Page 16 ©2019 Elekta Limited.

General safety and regulatory information
Safety precautions
Before use of new or unusual treatment techniques, it is the responsibility of the User to make
sure that:
• The equipment has the correct performance
• The equipment has the correct configuration
• The applicable software licenses are installed.
For more information on the above, contact your Elekta representative.
1.4.10 Safety precaution for cardiac pacemakers, other implants or portable
electronic medical devices
This is a safety precaution to prevent a malfunction of cardiac pacemakers, other implants or
portable electronic medical devices.
WARNING1.13
Do not put the cardiac pacemaker, other implant or portable electronic medical
device, in the radiation beam. If you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury.
WARNING1.14
Do not deliver radiation treatment without continuous checks on the operation of
the cardiac pacemaker, other implant or portable electronic medical device. If you
ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury.
A cardiac pacemaker, other implant or portable electronic medical device, can be damaged by very
small doses of radiation. Radiation treatment can make magnetic or electric fields, or ionizing
radiation, which can cause damage to these devices.
For more information on the necessary precautions, see the applicable documentation from the
manufacturer of the device.
1.4.11 Safety precautions for work with electrical and mechanical parts
The safety precautions for work with electrical and mechanical parts are the necessary safety
precautions to know to prevent fatal injury.
WARNING1.15
Do not start to do work on the linear accelerator before you isolate the power to
the work area at the interface cabinet. You can get a high voltage electric shock. If
you ignore this warning, it can cause fatal injury.
WARNING1.16
Do not remove the covers or cables from the equipment unless special instructions
in this document tell you to. The covers give access to high voltage electric parts.
Always install the covers and cables again before you use the equipment. If you
ignore this warning, it can cause fatal injury.
Elekta Medical Linear Accelerator Document ID:4513 370 2185 07
Corrective Maintenance Manual Technical Reference
©2019 Elekta Limited. PPaaggee 1177

General safety and regulatory information
Safety precautions
WARNING1.17
Do not remove the covers from the equipment unless special instructions in this
document tell you to. The covers give access to parts that move. Always install the
covers again before you use the equipment. If you ignore this warning, it can cause
fatal injury.
WARNING1.18
Be careful when the power supply switch of the equipment is put in the off
position. There continues to be a risk of high voltage electric shock. Some of the
customer interfaces can continue to supply electrical power to the electrical
terminals. If you ignore this warning, it can cause fatal injury.
Only operate the equipment in rooms that are in compliance with the applicable electrical safety
laws and regulations for this type of equipment.
Before you start the maintenance and repair work, or start to clean the equipment, isolate the
electrical power supply and prevent unwanted movement of the equipment.
1.4.12 Safety precautions for work with laser devices
The safety precautions for work with laser devices are the necessary safety precautions to know to
prevent serious injury to your eyes.
WARNING1.19
Do not look into the laser beams. The result can be damage to your eyes. If you
ignore this warning, it can cause serious injury.
1.4.13 Safety precaution for work with display screen equipment
This is a safety precaution to prevent damage to your health when you use the display screen
equipment.
WARNING1.20
Do not use the display screen equipment for long periods of time. If you ignore this
warning, you can cause injury.
When you use the display screen equipment (DSE), obey the applicable Health and Safety
regulations and procedures in the jurisdiction where you do the work.
Document ID: 4513 370 2185 07 Elekta Medical Linear Accelerator
Corrective Maintenance Manual Technical Reference
Page 18 ©2019 Elekta Limited.

General safety and regulatory information
Safety precautions
1.4.14 Safety precaution for the installation of accessories on the BLD
This is a safety precaution to prevent injury when you install an accessory on the BLD .
WARNING1.21
Do not touch the connector found in the accessory ring on the BLD. If you ignore
this warning, you can cause injury.
When you install an accessory, for example, a shadow tray, electron applicator, or front pointer, do
not touch the pins in the connector. The pins have a low electrical current, which can give an
electric shock when you touch them.
1.4.15 Safety precaution for work with high temperatures on the BLD covers
This is a safety precaution to prevent injury when you touch the beam limiting device or accessory
ring on the linear accelerator.
During normal operation, the maximum temperatures of the accessory ring and BLD outer covers
can increase to 45° C. Make sure that you do not touch the outer covers or the accessory ring for
more than 1 minute.
1.4.16 Precautions for work with electrostatic discharge sensitive devices
The precautions to prevent an electrostatic discharge (ESD) during work with ESD sensitive devices.
CAUTION1.1
Do not touch the PCBs and other electronic parts unless you wear a grounded
antistatic wrist strap. If you ignore this caution, you can cause damage to the
equipment.
CAUTION1.2
Do not touch the parts identified with the ESD caution label. Always obey the ESD
precautions during work on these parts. If you ignore this caution, you can cause
damage to the equipment.
Semiconductor devices, integrated circuits, and the parts that contain them, are ESD sensitive
devices. These devices are electrostatic discharge sensitive (ESDS) devices. ESD can cause damage
to these devices and cause them to have a malfunction immediately or some time after the
electrostatic discharge. To prevent the ESD damage to these devices, always wear a grounded
antistatic wrist strap, and obey the applicable ESD precautions and procedures.
Elekta recommends that all users receive the training about ESD. The training must include the ESD
caution label and these ESD precautions.

General safety and regulatory information
Safety precautions
1.4.17 Safety precaution for work with electrical and mechanical parts
This is a safety precaution to prevent fatal injury, or injury, by electrical and mechanical parts during
operation of the equipment.
WARNING1.22
Do not remove the cables or the covers, or operate the equipment without the
covers. If you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury.
The covers on the equipment prevent access to high voltage parts and parts that move. If you
remove the covers, there is a risk of high voltage shock or injury from parts that move.
Only remove the covers if you are an Elekta approved person with the correct authority and you
obey the applicable safety procedures.
1.4.18 Safety precautions for work with UV devices
The safety precautions to prevent serious injury to your eyes during work with UV devices.
WARNING1.23
Do not look into the ultraviolet lighting of the UV device without protective glasses.
If you ignore this warning, you can cause serious injury.
Some devices use ultraviolet lighting. The covers and screens of the device contain the ultraviolet
lighting to the inner parts of the device.
If there is damage to the covers or screen of the UV device, switch off the linear accelerator and
contact your Elekta representative.
1.4.19 Safety precaution for work on Agility™ BLDs with UV lighting

1.4.25 Safety precautions for the measurement of induced radioactivity
The safety precautions for the measurement of induced radioactivity are the necessary safety
precautions to know to prevent excessive exposure to radioactivity, which can cause serious injury
or damage to the environment.
Always use two radiation detectors to measure the level of induced radioactivity. Only use
radiation detectors that have an applicable calibration certificate. The period of time since the last
calibration date must be less than 12 months. Do not use the radiation detectors if the period of
time since the last calibration date is more than 12 months.
WARNING1.31
Do not move radioactive material (by air, sea, or land) unless you obey the
applicable laws and regulations for induced radioactivity and disposal of
radioactive material of the jurisdictions through which the movement occurs. If you
ignore this warning, it can cause serious injury and damage to the environment.

levels of induced radioactivity applicable in the United Kingdom.

jurisdiction in which the equipment is installed. The safe levels of radiation applicable in the UK do
not apply in all jurisdictions. If there are no safe levels available for a jurisdiction, contact Elekta for

1.4.34 Expected service life of the equipment
The expected service life is the length of time that the regular service and repair procedures can keep
the equipment safe to operate.
WARNING1.37
Do not operate the equipment unless you correctly complete the safety checks,
planned maintenance schedules, and extended life checks. If you ignore this
warning, you can cause fatal injury and damage to the equipment.
The expected service life of this equipment is 15 years of normal use.
The Elekta definition for 'expected service life' is the length of time that the regular service and
repair procedures can keep the equipment safe to operate.
The Elekta definition of 'normal use' is the treatment of 50 patients each day for five days each
week of the year.
The level of operation of the equipment can be different to ‘normal use’. This has an effect on the
length of the service life. Because of wear of parts, the risk of a malfunction can increase after 10
years operation. Elekta recommends that before you continue operation after 10 years, you
complete the regular extended life checks.
Elekta can help the User with the correct safety checks, planned maintenance schedules, and the
regular extended life checks for continued safe operation of the equipment.
The system must be maintained throughout its expected service life in compliance with the EMC
information detailed in the appendix in this document.
For information on the above checks and schedules, contact your local Elekta representative.
1.4.35 Disposal of the equipment
This is the procedure for the disposal of the equipment at End of Life to prevent damage to health
and the environment by radioactive and hazardous material.
WARNING1.38
Do not discard radioactive and hazardous material through the domestic waste
systems or in the environment. If you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury
and damage to the environment.
End of life (EOL) disposal is the procedure to disassemble, remove, and discard the equipment, in
full or in parts, to a condition where it is not possible to operate the equipment for its intended
use.
Always use procedures that give the best possible protection to the environment when you
remove and discard the equipment. The procedures must obey the laws and regulations of the
jurisdictions in which you discard the equipment. Do not discard Elekta equipment through the
domestic waste systems.
Only a Qualified Expert (QE) and an approved facility with an applicable license, must remove and
discard the equipment and recycle the material. Do not discard material that is hazardous to
health and the environment together with other material.
Before you remove and discard equipment, contact Elekta for information.

3.2 Navigating the linear accelerator corrective maintenance
manual
This Linear Accelerator Corrective Maintenance Manual has been designed in a modular format
that will eventually comprise a fully integrated, electronically published suite of information. The
main characteristics of modular documentation are that:
• Where possible, a piece of information resides in one place only - it is not duplicated and
is therefore easy to update.
• Categories of information (for example, repair instructions) always appear at the same
place in each module - so the information is easy to find.
• Cross-references are used to direct users to associated and/or more detailed
information.
The Linear Accelerator Corrective Maintenance Manual set therefore comprises a number of
modules, each of which is dedicated to a primary system of the linear accelerator. The modules are
compiled into three volumes as listed in Table3.1.
Table3.1 Volume structure of the Linear Accelerator Corrective Maintenance Manual set

1 Reference Manual (this module)

2

3 Cooling, Gas and Vacuum Systems

3.2.1 Architecture of the corrective maintenance manual modules
With the exception of this reference module, each module of the Linear Accelerator Corrective
Maintenance Manual set conforms to a similar architecture that allows users to drill down through
the document set at need. A high-level system overview leads to a suite of detailed technical
descriptions followed by troubleshooting, repair and post-repair instructions. In general, each
module:
• Chapter 1 always contains important regulatory and safety information which are
mandatory requirements of technical medical documentation. The chapter also includes
a comprehensive glossary of acronyms and abbreviations, and a list of the
cross-references to all other modules in the document set.
• Chapter 2 provides an overview of the subject system(s) and summarizes the system
functions within the linear accelerator. The narrative is usually supported by a schematic
diagram of the system(s).

Safety information for service personnel
3.3 Safety information for service personnel
This section presents the complex safety issues that apply when an Elekta linear accelerator is
serviced. Warnings of specific hazards also appear at the appropriate point of application
throughout the entire Linear Accelerator Corrective Maintenance Manual set.
3.3.1 General safety
The linear accelerator is Class 1, Type B equipment according to IEC 60601-2-1, Part2C, Computer
Controlled Radiotherapy Equipment.
To make sure that all service personnel work in a safe way, all warnings and cautions throughout
the Linear Accelerator Corrective Maintenance Manual must be strictly obeyed.
The use of this equipment should be controlled by qualified members of the hospital staff, whose
responsibilities include:
The assignment of passwords to suitable persons for each of the various levels of
authorization.
Making sure that all persons who work on the linear accelerator are competent to perform
the procedures for which they are authorized.
The equipment should be used only by service personnel who are trained in the use of locally
applicable regulations, standards and laws that govern the safe use of ionizing radiation, and in the
specific use of this equipment.
Service personnel must be familiar with the contents of this document before attempting any
procedure and/or instruction given in the Linear Accelerator Corrective Maintenance Manual set.
Service personnel must use the equipment only as described in this manual, and be aware of all
warnings given in this manual.
Elekta provides training courses for service personnel, which cover the safe use of this equipment.
The members of the hospital staff who control the use of the linear accelerator must arrange for
the regular maintenance of the equipment as stated in the operation and service manuals.
3.3.2 Powering up and powering down the linear accelerator
3.3.2.1 Emergency switches
Service personnel must be thoroughly familiar with the location and function of all emergency
switches associated with the linear accelerator. These include:
• The STOP button on the handheld controller(s) in the treatment room.
• The emergency stop motors button on each user interface of the treatment table in the
treatment room.
• All locally installed emergency power OFF switches in the treatment room, the control
room and the equipment room.
3.3.2.2 Powering up and powering down the linear accelerator
Usually, each hospital has a local procedure for powering up and powering down an Elekta linear
accelerator and, if so, the local procedure should always be used. In the absence of local
procedures, follow the instructions given in the applicable manual of Instructions for User - Service
Mode for the TCS.

3.3.3 Fire hazards
WARNING3.1
There continues to be a risk of high voltage electric shock after the power supply
switch of the equipment is put in the OFF position. Some of the customer interfaces
can continue to supply electrical power to the electrical terminals. This can give you

The HT discharge rod (Brinckley Hook) is stowed on the side of the modulator (Area 30). The

Elekta linear accelerators
3.4 Elekta linear accelerators
Elekta linear accelerators are machines for use in clinical radiation therapy. They produce a beam
of high energy particles that is accurately delivered to the target tumor in a patient reclining on a
treatment table. A high tension (HT) and radio frequency (RF) source provides the energy that
accelerates the particles. Travelling wave technology is used to transport the radiation beam, and a
beam limiting device (BLD) with collimation is used to shape the beam and deliver it precisely to
the target.
The linear accelerator is a drum and gantry structure that rotates through 360° around an
isocentric axis. The components that produce the radiation beam are mounted on the drum and
the gantry. The machine is the main component in a complete system for the delivery of
therapeutic radiation to patients. The other parts of the system are:
• Treatment table
• Beam limiting device (BLD)
• Control system
• Imaging systems.
The linear accelerator is usually installed in a suite of three rooms which contain the following
main parts:
• Treatment room - gantry, BLD, treatment table, monitors, handheld controllers, imaging
system components, covers, wall-mounted lasers.
• Control room - linear accelerator control system, function keypad, imaging system
workstations.
• Equipment room - gantry drum and all other equipment such as the interface cabinet,
reeling post, gas cylinder, table electrical interface module (EIM), client’s interface
terminal board (CITB) and so on.
The arrangement of a typical digital accelerator suite is represented in Figure3.1.

• Beam Modulator™
• Agility™.

Service Mode.
The control system is not a main topic in this Digital Accelerator Corrective Maintenance Manual
but is fully described in a dedicated document set. However, the Service Mode is used extensively

systems are not topics in this Digital Accelerator Corrective Maintenance Manual but are fully
described in dedicated document sets.
3.4.5 Clinical beams
The digital accelerator generates two types of clinical beam:
• Electron - used to treat superficial tumors (less than 5 cm deep). The therapeutic energy
range for electrons is 4MeV to 22 MeV.
• X-ray (photon) - used to treat deep-seated tumors. The therapeutic energy range of
X-rays is 4 MV to 25 MV. Typically, X-ray beams are used in combinations (for example,
opposed pairs), which deliver very high doses to the target, but with relatively low skin
doses. X-ray beams provide up to three energy levels.

3.5 Brief description of the digital accelerator assembly
The digital accelerator itself comprises the following main assemblies:
• Gantry drum
• Gantry arm
• Interface cabinet
• Cable reeling post.
Gantry drum
The gantry drum is housed in the equipment room. Subunits of the digital accelerator are mounted
in dedicated areas within the gantry drum and at the rear of the gantry drum.
Gantry arm
The gantry arm projects from the gantry drum into the treatment room. The arm contains the
linear accelerating waveguide that transports the radiation beam from its source in the gantry
drum to the BLD mounted at the treatment end of the gantry arm.
Interface cabinet
The interface cabinet is located in the equipment room. It provides the electrical interconnection
between all the systems and subsystems of the digital accelerator.
Cable reeling post
The cable reeling post is located in the equipment room. It feeds and retracts the main cable loom
to and from the gantry drum as the drum rotates. The reeling post also provides connections to
external services such as cooling water and dielectric gas.

3.5.1 Principle of operation
An Elekta digital accelerator comprises a number of primary systems:
• HT and RF
• Beam physics
• Power supplies and Interlocks
• Dosimetry
• Movements
• Cooling, gas and vacuum
• System communications
• Covers
• Accessories.
3.5.1.1 HT and RF systems
The HT and RF systems operating together produce a radiation beam of high-energy electrons or
X-rays (photons).
Stable, high-voltage pulses are produced by the HT system. In the RF system, the HT pulses cause
the generation of a travelling RF wave that is used to transport electrons through an accelerating
waveguide and into the BLD.
The travelling RF wave is produced by a modulator that is powered by the high-voltage pulses from
the HT system. A magnetron in the modulator generates an oscillating RF power input to the
waveguide and thus forms the travelling RF wave. An electron gun, powered by the same HT
pulses as the magnetron, injects electrons into the waveguide where they are transported by the
RF wave.
The compact design of the digital accelerator is made possible by the use of the high-power
magnetron as the RF power source.
See Digital Accelerator Corrective Maintenance Manual - High Tension and Radio Frequency
Systems for a full description of the HT and RF systems.
3.5.1.2 Beam physics system
The RF energy is propagated along the accelerating waveguide as travelling waves. Energy is
transferred from the travelling waves to the electrons, which are thus accelerated to emerge at the
output of the waveguide with considerable gain in energy. The travelling waves of RF and electron
energy are transported along their path by a set of magnets that surround the accelerating
waveguide.
The high-energy electron beam passes through an achromatic bending magnet system that
deflects the beam through different angles until it finally emerges at the target/window. Here the
beam strikes a target that generates X-rays, or passes through a thin metal window to produce
electron radiation. Beyond the target/window area, the radiation beam passes to the BLD.
See Digital Accelerator Corrective Maintenance Manual - Beam Physics System for a full
description of the beam physics system.

3.5.1.3 Power supplies and Interlocks
Power supplies
The digital accelerator requires electrical power supplies for a number of systems and subsystems:
• Main three-phase supply for the HT and other systems
• Low voltage (LV) power supplies for various magnet solenoids such as the Magnetron
magnet, the focus coils and the coarse and fine bending magnets
• Low tension (LT) power supplies for the control areas and motor drive areas
• Interlock system power supplies.
Three-phase electrical power is obtained from the hospital main supply. The separate power
supplies for the magnet solenoids are produced by the low voltage power supply unit (LVPSU)
assembly. The control areas, the motor drive areas and the interlock system relays are supplied by
local power supply units.
Interlock system
The digital accelerator is potentially hazardous. To protect patients, clinical staff, service
technicians and others in the vicinity of the machine, a number of hardware, firmware and
software interlocks are fitted.
The principle of operation of all the interlock chains is similar. The hardware of each chain is a
serial circuit consisting of relays, contactors and PCBs in all three control areas. The inputs to the
interlock chains come from the TCC (software interlocks), from local PCB programming (firmware
interlocks), and from sensing devices such as temperature and pressure switches.
Each input imposes one or more operating or safety conditions on its respective interlock chain. If
any condition is violated, the affected interlock chain is broken and the operation of the digital
accelerator is partially or entirely inhibited while the unsafe condition prevails.
There are six hardware interlock chains:
• Main
• Low tension (LT)
• Movements
• HT
• Pulse repetition frequency (PRF)
• Client.
With the exception of the PRF interlock, the hardware interlocks all control the contactors which
supply mains electrical power to different systems and subsystems of the digital accelerator. The
PRF interlock system directly inhibits the generation of radiation if an unsafe condition occurs.
The HT, movements and PRF interlocks all have dual circuits (primary and secondary).
See Digital Accelerator Corrective Maintenance Manual - Power Supplies and Interlock Systems for
a full description of the power supplies and interlocks.
3.5.1.4 Dosimetry system
The dosimetry system controls and monitors the radiation dose delivered by the digital
accelerator. It also monitors the ambient temperature and atmospheric pressure during
treatment. All three measurements are made by dual circuits (channel A and channel B), and the
readings are compared by the TCC. A number of dosimetry interlocks prevent the delivery of
radiation in the event of a fault or error.
Radiation is detected by an ionization (ion) chamber in the path of the radiation beam, which feeds
data to both dosimetry channels. The system includes an arrangement to monitor the flatness of
the radiation field, and these signals provide software supervision of the beam control servos.

See Digital Accelerator Corrective Maintenance Manual - Dosimetry System for a full description of
the dosimetry system.
3.5.1.5 Movement systems
The moving assemblies discussed in this manual are the gantry of the digital accelerator and the
BLD. With the exception of rotation, other movements of the BLD are discussed in Corrective
Maintenance Manuals for Beam Limiting Devices.
The treatment table movement system is described in the Precise Treatment Table Corrective
Maintenance Manual.
The gantry has a dedicated movement system that controls the rotation of the gantry drum. The
BLD has a dedicated movement system that controls the rotation of the entire assembly. These are
known collectively as machine movements.
The principle of operation of each movement system is essentially the same. The moving assembly
is driven by one or more electric motors. The demand for a movement is given by the user at a
manual controller. The movement is enabled, and its speed and direction are set by the TCC. These
commands are processed in the respective control areas and motor drive areas of the digital
accelerator to provide drive signals to the appropriate motor.
Encoders monitor the actual position of the moving assembly, and a servo loop returns this
information to the TCC, which can therefore start and stop the movement, and dynamically adjust
the speed and direction of motion in real time. The positional data is also used by the TCC to
prevent the assembly from being driven beyond predetermined limits.
See Digital Accelerator Corrective Maintenance Manual - Movement Systems for a full description
of the movements system.
3.5.1.6 Cooling, gas and vacuum systems
Various components of the digital accelerator operate at high temperatures. The machine is
therefore equipped with a cooling water system comprising:
An external (primary) water circuit that receives cold water from, and returns warm water to,
the hospital’s water facility
An internal (secondary) water circuit - a closed loop with a heat exchanger that transfers heat
to the external return circuit.
The dielectric gas is used to cool the magnetron and waveguide windows and prevent arcing in the
rectangular section of the waveguide. The gas supply is obtained from a conventional cylinder
located in or near the reeling post.
The accelerating waveguide is evacuated to make sure that electrons are not obstructed or
deflected by collisions with gas molecules. The vacuum is maintained by two ion pumps - one at
the gun end of the waveguide and the other at the target end.
See Digital Accelerator Corrective Maintenance Manual - Cooling, Gas and Vacuum Systems for full
descriptions of the cooling, gas and vacuum systems.
3.5.1.7 System communications
The digital accelerator is controlled by the software that resides in the TCC, and the TCC
communicates with the digital accelerator via two serial links (A and B). At the digital accelerator,
the serial links connect to three separate control areas:
Radiation head (BLD) control area (RHCA)
HT control area (HTCA)
Interface cabinet control area (ICCA).

Brief description of the digital accelerator assembly
The control areas distribute command and data signals from the TCC to the other systems of the
digital accelerator, and return data and other information to the TCC. These signals are known as
items.
Each control area consists of a PCB rack with a backplane and a set of PCBs, and is divided into two
remote terminal units (RTU A and RTU B). Each RTU has integral power supplies, so that the
interlocks and safety circuits handled by each RTU operate independently.
Within each control area, the two RTUs communicate with each other over a dedicated bus. The
backplane provides the interconnection between the control areas and the other systems of the
digital accelerator.
See Digital Accelerator Corrective Maintenance Manual - System Communications for a full
description of the system communications.
3.5.1.8 Covers
All digital accelerators are fitted with a set of fiberglass covers which:
• Protect patients and operators from hazardous components and conditions within the
gantry arm and the BLD
• Protect the components of the digital accelerator in the treatment room from
inadvertent damage in use
• Present a pleasing and unthreatening aspect to patients in the treatment room.
Covers, and a supporting frame, provide the dividing wall between the treatment room and the
equipment room. Within the treatment room, the gantry arm of the digital accelerator is entirely
covered and, except for the treatment field, the BLD is also covered.
The cover sets for the digital accelerators are different in construction and color. See Digital
Accelerator Corrective Maintenance Manual - Covers for more information.
3.5.1.9 Accessories
The usual accessories supplied with Elekta digital accelerators are:
• Applicators
• Shadow trays
• Front pointer
• Optical distance meter.
Applicators are attached to the BLD when required by the prescription. They are used with
electron beam treatments to attain a uniform, sharply-defined field of the required dimensions.
Shadow trays are used with X-ray treatments to position blocks in the treatment field. This
provides final beam shaping and enables the safe delivery of irregularly shaped fields.
The front pointer is a mechanical assembly that attaches to an accessory ring on the BLD. It
indicates the isocenter of the radiation beam.
The optical distance meter is attached beneath the gantry arm. The device projects a scale that
indicate the source-to-surface distance (SSD) over a defined range.
See Digital Accelerator Corrective Maintenance Manual - Accessories for a full description of the
digital accelerator accessories.

3.7 Machine states
The digital accelerator can exist in a limited number of stable states only. Each state defines the
number of systems to which electrical power is applied. The higher the machine state, the greater
the number of systems that are powered up.
The switching of electrical power to the systems is accomplished via the hardware interlock chains,
which have software and firmware inputs. Each interlock chain applies power to a number of
circuits.
A flat interlock structure is used - as distinct from a tree structure. The flat structure employs a
small number of interlock chains that pass through each control area, with numerous relay
contacts and monitoring points.
Control of the interlock system is based on a 'state of readiness' regime in which the digital
accelerator occupies one of a relatively small number of states (known as a superstate). In each
superstate, a fixed hardware state is imposed on the remote terminal units (RTUs) in each control
area - for example, the appropriate contactors are enabled. This imposed condition is known as
the global state. Firmware on certain PCBs in each control area receives a global state code from
the TCC, which sets the global state of the addressed RTU.
The digital accelerator changes state automatically during power up, and normally waits in the
Preparatory state. If the software detects an error condition, the state of the digital accelerator
suspends to a lower level to prevent damage to the machine, or risk to the user or patient.
Users can force the digital accelerator to change certain states. See Instructions for Use - Service
Mode for the TCS for information on displaying the machine states and forcing the user-selectable
states.

4.7.2.5 READY indication (optional)
A Ready indication provides a closing contact to allow indication that the linear accelerator is in
the Ready to Start state, with the LT contactors, and movements system contactors enabled.
The Ready lamp uses contacts PL73E-14 and PL73E-15 and relay RL7 on the IRC-74B PCB. The input
is normally open, which allows a client's supply to be used together with a warning lamp. The
warnings in the equipment room and the treatment room indicate the transfer of the machine to
the Ready to Start state.
Relay RL7 on the IRC-74B PCB is energized from the contacts of relay RLA2 on the ROC-ICB PCB by a
signal from a programmable logic device (PLD) on the DIE-ICB PCB. The PLD is activated when item
i495 (Global State ICB) is received.
4.7.2.6 Radiation warning lamp
The radiation warning lamp output circuit is powered whenever the HT system is energized. The
circuit uses contacts on primary HT1 contactor CON-J, the secondary HT2 contactor CON-K and the
HT soft start contactor CON-D to supply power to the RADIATION ON or RADIATION OFF warning
lamps.
The RADIATION ON warning lamp is illuminated when:
CON-D is energized
or
CON-J and CON-K are energized.
The RADIATION OFF warning lamp is illuminated when
CON-D and CON-J are de-energized
or
CON-D and CON-K are de-energized.
The supply for the lamps is provided by the client.
4.7.2.7 Room lights
The level of intensity of the room lights is controlled by relay RL6 on the IRC-74B PCB. The relay
contacts are connected to:
PL73E-18 - normally open
PL73E-19 - common
PL73E-20 - normally closed
The contacts route the full power to the room lights, or supply the room lights through a dimmer
switch.
Relay RL6 on the IRC-74B PCB is energized by transistor TR5 on the ROC-ICB PCB when item i325
(Lights dim) is received. Item i325 is generated in response to i13 (Light request), which is
generated when the ROOM LIGHTS key on the HHC is pressed.

5.1.3 Dosimetry performance data
5.1.3.1 Type
Dual-channel dose monitoring system that uses independent ionization chambers. The two
channels are designated channel A and channel B.
5.1.3.2 Definition of monitor units
1 monitor unit (MU) is approximately equal to 1cGy.
5.1.3.3 Normal termination
The set dose equals the dose delivered in segment MUs in channel A.
5.1.3.4 Abnormal terminations
An abnormal termination occurs when:
• The dose monitored by channel B exceeds the set dose by 10MU.
• The doses monitored by channel A and channel B differ by more than 2MU or 2%,
whichever is the greater.
• The preset treatment time has elapsed.
• The set value of beam MUs is reached.
• The channel A dose rate exceeds 1200MU/minute (25% greater than nominal, for slow
excursions).
• The absorbed dose-rate at the normal treatment distance is greater than 125% or less
than 75% of the nominal value over a period of 5 seconds. In this event, a software dose
rate interlock terminates irradiation.
5.1.3.5 Reproducibility ()
Better than ±0.5% for electron and X-ray modes.
5.1.3.6 Proportionality
In a series of irradiations in the range of 50MU/minute to the maximum dose rate, and for doses
distributed over the range 50MU to 1000MU, the proportionality of an accumulated dose as
measured by an external dosemeter at the isocenter) is within ±1% or 1 MU, whichever is greater.
5.1.3.7 Linearity
The proportionality specification given in Section5.1.3.6 applies to the normal range of preset
dose rates. Under these conditions, the linear accelerator delivers a constant calibrated dose per
pulse.
Under fault conditions it may be possible to achieve a variable dose per pulse. The condition can
be simulated by selecting manual gun in Service Mode, when the delivered dose error will not
exceed ±5% of the indicated dose for dose rates up to twice the specified maximum.
5.1.3.8 Dependence on position of linear accelerator assembly
For any angle of gantry or BLD, the precision of the dosimetry system is ±1% or 1MU, whichever is
greater.

5.7 Accessories
5.7.1 Loading conditions
WARNING5.1
Do not attach end frames with a weight of more than 7 kg. If you ignore this
warning, it can cause fatal injury, clinical mistreatment, or damage to the
equipment.
WARNING5.2
Do not put more than 30 kg of shielding blocks on to a shadow tray for gantry
angles of 0° or 180°. For all other gantry angles, do not put more than 15 kg of
shielding blocks on to a shadow tray. If you ignore this warning, it can cause fatal
injury or damage to the equipment.
WARNING5.3
Make sure that you only use the accessories attached to the BLD in their safe work
limits. If you ignore this warning, it can cause fatal injury, clinical mistreatment, or
damage to the equipment.
The conditions for safe work limits for accessories attached to the BLD are:
1 A force greater than 250N must not be applied on the hook or latch on the accessory ring.
2 Accessories must weigh less than 50kg.
3 The center of mass of an accessory must not be more than 80mm from the BLD accessory
ring.
5.7.2 BLD accessories
5.7.2.1 Field defining system
The optical projection system with an indication of the field center by crosswires and wedge filter
orientation. The system maintains an accuracy of 1mm at 100cm and 2mm at floor level.
5.7.2.2 Distance meter
Optical distance meter which projects a scale to indicate the SSD over the range 75cm to 170cm
(accuracy ±3mm).
5.7.2.3 Touchguard
A guard ring fitted around the BLD aperture, which inhibits movements if the ring touches
anything in the path of the moving BLD.
It should be noted that the BLD is effectively extended when a shadow tray is fitted and therefore
the touchguard no longer provides touch protection.
